guest: Mac (Mac the VC / Marlon 'Mac' — Rare Breed Ventures)
guest_role: Founder and Managing Partner of Rare Breed Ventures, a pre-seed fund
assertiveness_why: Harry asks warm, well-prepared follow-ups and invites Mac to voice grievances, but he never contests or challenges anything Mac says, mostly amplifying and agreeing.
question_style: Almost entirely open-ended and biographical, built to let the guest expand ('what else do you think is fucked up in venture?'); Harry rarely interjects his own thesis and never pushes back, though he does layer follow-ups that dig for specifics like ratios and mechanics.
tone: Extremely matey and fan-like, calling Mac 'man' repeatedly, expressing open admiration ('huge pleasure', 'I absolutely love that'), high energy and enthusiastic throughout.
depth: Mostly surface/narrative (life story, fundraising journey) but does probe some technical fund-mechanics details: check sizes, reserve strategy, rolling close cadence, 506(c) solicitation, and rolling funds vs traditional fund structure trade-offs.
harry_quotes: How did you make your way into venture?
Do you think the traditional fundraising process is fucked up?
What else do you think is fucked up in venture?
How diverse in terms of concentration? How do you think about check size?
learnings: Mac built LP trust and education almost entirely through direct Twitter outreach, DMing any VC who followed him and converting roughly three-quarters of those meetings into useful relationships.
He deliberately rejected typical fund conventions (GP commit, traditional capital call timing, minimum close thresholds), designing bespoke LP payment options and a three-week rolling close instead.
He argues 'hot deals' (led by brand-name funds) are conflated with 'good deals' by LPs, when only actual returns should matter for an emerging pre-seed manager.
Institutional diversity commitments after 2020 rang hollow to him because large pension-fund LPs structurally can't write small enough checks to back first-time diverse emerging managers.

He avoided rolling funds because their quarterly-cohort structure shortchanges LPs who aren't active in the specific quarter a standout investment gets made; he replicated the same speed/access benefits inside a traditional 506(c) fund instead.
His investment thesis prioritizes maximum check size as early as possible (pre-seed/seed) since multiples on capital are highest at that stage and shrink at each later round.
notable: Guest self-identifies as 'Mac the VC,' a pseudonymous/persona-branded emerging fund manager whose entire fundraise (from $400K to $10M) was conducted via Twitter DMs during the pandemic.
